confidence.disclosure: Ali ibn Musa al-Ridha, the eighth Twelver Shia imam, is documented in the major Shia biographical works (al-Kulayni's al-Kafi, al-Mufid's Kitab al-Irshad, al-Tabarsi's Ilam al-Wara) as well as in Sunni chronicles for his unprecedented involvement in Abbasid imperial politics. His birth and early life in Medina, his summons by Caliph al-Mamun to Khurasan in 816, his designation as heir-apparent, and his death at Tus in 818 are firmly documented; the date of his death is given by the major Shia sources as 23 Dhu al-Qa'da 203 AH (26 May 818).

journey.event: Born in Medina on 11 Dhu al-Qa'da 148 AH (29 December 765), the son of the seventh imam Musa al-Kazim and a freedwoman known as Umm al-Banin. Reared in Medina in the household of his father, who is then the recognised head of the Husaynid line.
Educated by his father in the religious sciences and the imami tradition. Becomes known among the Shia community of Medina for his learning, his eloquence, and his ascetic temperament.

On the death of his father Musa al-Kazim - who has died in the prison of Caliph Harun al-Rashid in Baghdad - succeeds him as the eighth imam of the Twelver line. Most of the Shia community of Medina, Iraq, and Khurasan accepts him, though a faction (the Waqifiyya) refuses to acknowledge his father's death and rejects his imamate.

Spends the next sixteen years in Medina as the recognised imam, teaching, receiving disciples, and gathering the customary religious dues (khums) from the Shia communities of the empire. Lives quietly under the surveillance of Caliphs Harun al-Rashid and (after 809) al-Amin and al-Mamun, but is not personally persecuted as his father had been.

In 816 Caliph al-Mamun, who has been ruling from Marv in Khurasan since defeating his brother al-Amin in the civil war, summons Ali al-Ridha to his court. The summons is unprecedented - no Husaynid imam has ever been called to court in this way - and the imam, after some hesitation, complies.

Travels from Medina via Basra and Ahwaz, taking the southern route in order to avoid Kufa - a precaution against any Shia uprising that might use his presence as a rallying point. The journey takes some months and is marked by stories of miraculous events at every stage, later collected in Shia hagiography.

Arrives at the caliphal court at Marv in eastern Khurasan, where al-Mamun has established his administration. Received with extraordinary honour by the caliph, who installs him in a palace and orders the courtiers to defer to him in all things.

On 24 March 817 (2 Ramadan 201 AH) al-Mamun formally designates Ali al-Ridha as his heir-apparent (wali al-ahd) and orders the official colour of the dynasty changed from Abbasid black to Alid green. Coins are struck in al-Ridha's name and the imam is married to al-Mamun's daughter Umm Habib. The act is the most extraordinary gesture toward the Alids ever made by an Abbasid caliph.

The designation provokes outrage in Baghdad, where the Abbasid family proclaim al-Mamun deposed and elevate his uncle Ibrahim ibn al-Mahdi as rival caliph. In Marv, al-Ridha continues to participate in court ceremonies and to hold theological debates with the leading scholars of all religions assembled by al-Mamun, debates which become a famous subject of Shia literature.

In early 818 al-Mamun decides to march back to Baghdad to reassert his authority. Ali al-Ridha accompanies the imperial caravan westward through Khurasan. At Sarakhs the imam is reported to be unwell.

Dies suddenly at the village of Sanabad near Tus on 23 Dhu al-Qa'da 203 AH (26 May 818), aged 53. Shia tradition firmly holds that he was poisoned by al-Mamun, who feared that his designation of an Alid heir had become politically untenable; some Sunni sources accept this and others attribute the death to natural causes (a fever after eating grapes). Buried at Sanabad in the same complex as the grave of Caliph Harun al-Rashid (who had died at Tus in 809). The site grows into the great shrine city of Mashhad ('the place of martyrdom'), the most important Shia pilgrimage site in Iran and the spiritual heart of Khurasan.
